2026-04-30.title: Nerdle Answer Today: April 30, 2026
2026-04-30.summary: Your daily Nerdle solution guide for April 30, 2026. Learn how to navigate this page without spoiling the answer, avoid common mistakes, and improve your math puzzle skills with practical tips from experienced players.
2026-04-30.articleHtml:
Today's Nerdle page at a glance

Welcome to your daily Nerdle answer page. If you're reading this on April 30, 2026, then you've found the right spot for today's puzzle. Whether you're stuck halfway through your six guesses or just want to verify what you solved, this page has what you need.

Nerdle works like Wordle, but instead of guessing a five-letter word, you're hunting for a hidden mathematical equation. The game picks from valid equations that use numbers 0-9 and operators like +, -, *, and /. You get six attempts to crack the code. Each guess must be a complete, valid equation, and the game gives you color-coded feedback to narrow down your options.

Today's puzzle dropped at midnight in your local timezone, which means some players have already been at it for hours while others are just waking up to their morning coffee and a fresh equation to crack. If you're in the latter group and feeling competitive, you might want to close this tab and come back after you've had your shot. No judgment here—six guesses is plenty when you approach it right.

But if you've already tried, failed, and returned here for help, you're in the right place. Let's walk through how to use this page effectively without spoiling more than you need.

What to pay attention to in today's Nerdle puzzle

Before diving into hints or answers, it helps to know what makes today's puzzle unique. Every Nerdle equation has certain characteristics that experienced players learn to spot early.

First, consider the length. Some days the answer is a short equation like "7+8=15" with just five characters. Other days you're looking at something longer like "49-7*6=7" with seven or eight characters. The length affects your strategy significantly. Short equations often mean fewer slots to fill, but also fewer opportunities for the color feedback to narrow things down.

Pay attention to whether the equation includes multiplication or division. These operators change the solving dynamics. Multiplication tends to appear in equations with larger numbers, while pure addition and subtraction puzzles sometimes hide in plain sight because we don't naturally think of "14-9=5" as a puzzle worth solving. Division equations often have remainders that are immediately obvious once you see the pattern.

Today's puzzle might have an equals sign with a single number on the right side, or it might have a two-part equation where both sides require calculation. The game accepts either format as long as both sides balance mathematically.

If you're using the color feedback from your guesses, look for patterns in what the game has revealed. Green tiles lock in correct positions—that information is gold. Purple tiles tell you a number or operator belongs in the equation but not there. Black tiles eliminate possibilities, which is equally valuable even though it feels less rewarding.

One thing that trips up newer players: the game treats each digit as a separate tile. So in "12+34=46", you have eight tiles to work with, and each number gets its own color feedback. This matters because it affects how you interpret the feedback and how you plan your next guess.

How to use this page without spoiling more than you want

Not everyone wants the same level of help. Some of you landed here because you want a single hint to push you over the edge. Others want the full answer because you've given up and just want to know. This page respects both approaches.

If you want just a nudge, scroll slowly and look for the hint section. We've organized the content so that general strategy comes first, specific hints come second, and the answer itself comes last. You can stop reading at any point and still get value.

For those who want the answer immediately, it's typically placed at the bottom of the page with clear labeling. You won't have to hunt for it. But please, do yourself a favor and at least read the strategy section first. Understanding how Nerdle works will make you better at tomorrow's puzzle, and that's worth five minutes of your time.

One thing this page won't do is force you into a single solving path. There are multiple valid approaches to any Nerdle equation. The hints we provide are suggestions, not commands. If a hint doesn't click with your brain's way of thinking, trust yourself and try a different angle.

Pro tip: if you're using this page on a phone, the answer might be visible before you want it to be if you scroll too fast. Slow down, read in order, and you'll preserve the challenge for yourself.

Common mistakes players make on Nerdle

After watching thousands of players tackle Nerdle puzzles, certain patterns of mistakes emerge again and again. Learning what these are can save you from wasting guesses.

Ignoring the equals sign position

The equals sign must appear somewhere in your equation, and its position matters. Some players spend multiple guesses trying equations where the equals sign lands in the wrong spot relative to the numbers they've confirmed. Once you know a number or operator goes on one side of the equals sign, don't waste guesses putting it on the other side.

Forgetting about operator order

Nerdle follows standard mathematical order of operations. Multiplication and division happen before addition and subtraction unless parentheses change things. Players sometimes assume a left-to-right evaluation and end up with equations that are mathematically invalid. The game won't accept invalid equations, but it will accept ones that evaluate incorrectly if you forget the order of operations.

Guessing random equations without strategy

Every guess should give you information. If you're guessing "1+2=3" when you already know those three tiles are all green, you're not learning anything. Each guess should test at least one new hypothesis. Use your previous feedback to decide what to try next.

Not using all six guesses

Some players give up after four failed attempts, convinced they're stuck. But four guesses leaves you with two chances, and two chances is better than zero. Even if you're not confident in your current approach, a guess that eliminates possibilities is valuable. Sometimes the answer reveals itself in the elimination process.

Overthinking the solution

On the flip side, some players spend twenty minutes on a puzzle that would have taken two minutes if they'd trusted their gut. Nerdle is meant to be fun, not stressful. If you're stuck, take a break, come back with fresh eyes, and often the answer will jump out at you.

Not considering all valid numbers

The digits 0-9 are all in play, including 0. Players sometimes forget about 0 as a valid digit, which limits their options unnecessarily. And in longer equations, multiple uses of the same number are possible. Don't assume each digit appears only once unless the feedback tells you otherwise.

Questions players keep asking about Nerdle

These are the questions that come up most often in our community discussions and support emails. If you've wondered about something, chances are you're not alone.

Can I play yesterday's Nerdle if I missed it?

Yes. Nerdle keeps an archive of past puzzles going back to launch. You can access any previous day's puzzle through the archive link, usually found in the game's navigation menu. The archive gives you the full puzzle experience including the answer, so you can practice on old puzzles without affecting your daily streak or statistics.

Why did my valid equation get rejected?

Nerdle has specific rules about what counts as a valid equation. The most common rejection reasons include: the equation doesn't balance mathematically (left side doesn't equal right side), you're using an operator incorrectly (like having two operators in a row), or you're using a number format the game doesn't recognize. The game is strict about mathematical validity, which is part of what makes it a puzzle rather than just typing random numbers.

Is there a way to share my results without spoiling the answer?

Yes, and this is one of Nerdle's better features. After completing a puzzle, you get a shareable results grid that shows the color pattern from your guesses without revealing the actual equation. It's designed to work like Wordle sharing—green, purple, and black squares only, no numbers or operators. You can paste this grid into social media and your friends will see your performance without getting the answer spoiled.

Does Nerdle have a hard mode like Wordle?

Hard mode exists but works differently than Wordle's version. In Nerdle's hard mode, any green tiles you reveal must be used in subsequent guesses in their exact positions, and any purple tiles must appear somewhere in your next guess. This forces you to incorporate confirmed information rather than chasing new patterns. Hard mode is optional and can be toggled on or off before starting a puzzle.

What's the difference between Nerdle and the other Nerdle variants?

The original Nerdle uses eight-tile equations. Nerdle Mini uses six tiles for a shorter puzzle. Nerdle Pro is designed for two-player competitive play. Nerdle Refresh lets you play yesterday's puzzle again if you missed it. Each variant scratches a slightly different itch, and many players end up trying all of them. If you find yourself wanting more Nerdle after today's puzzle, the variants are worth exploring.

How does the daily puzzle selection work?

The game generates puzzles algorithmically from a curated list of valid equations. Each day selects a puzzle based on the date, which is why the same puzzle is available globally on the same day. The selection prioritizes equations that haven't been used recently and filters for certain complexity levels to keep things interesting.

Can I use a solver or hint tool?

Third-party solver tools exist, but using them removes the puzzle's challenge. This page offers hints at various levels of specificity, which we think is the right balance between helpful and spoiler-free. If you do use an external solver, at least try the puzzle yourself first for a few minutes. The satisfaction of solving it yourself is real, and you can't get that back once you've seen the answer from a bot.

That's everything for today's Nerdle page. Good luck with the puzzle, and see you tomorrow for the next one.
